does: This workshop convenes interdisciplinary researchers to advance bidirectional human-AI alignment through value-centered design and reciprocal co-adaptation. Rather than treating alignment as a one-way process of conforming AI to fixed human values, it positions alignment as a dynamic interaction where humans and AI systems mutually influence and adapt to each other. The workshop brings together perspectives from HCI, AI safety, social sciences and related fields to develop methods for embedding human and societal values into AI systems while enabling humans to critically engage with and learn from AI. It organizes participants around three core activities: exploring interactive alignment methods that support real-time value negotiation between humans and systems, designing frameworks for evaluating societal impact beyond narrow metrics, and developing alignment strategies that remain robust as contexts and relationships evolve over time.
connects: This work builds on established alignment research that has traditionally focused on specifying and optimizing AI systems toward predetermined human values, extending it through dialogue with human-centered AI and participatory design traditions. It responds to limitations in unidirectional alignment approaches by incorporating insights from HCI about technology's social embeddedness and from social science about values' contextual and contested nature. The workshop connects to growing recognition that value alignment cannot be treated as a static, one-time problem but must account for genuine human-AI interaction dynamics. It bridges conversations from prior CHI and ICLR workshops on bidirectional alignment, synthesizing technical AI safety methods with qualitative approaches to understanding how people actually encounter and negotiate with AI systems in practice.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward AI systems that genuinely support human agency rather than simply automating human preferences. By centering reciprocal adaptation, it enables future research on interactive alignment mechanisms where values can be clarified, contested, and refined through use rather than specified upfront. This approach supports development of AI systems that remain aligned even as societal values shift or as individuals encounter values different from their own. The workshop establishes foundations for evaluating alignment success through richer metrics capturing human flourishing, dignity, and meaningful choice rather than narrow compliance measures. It positions alignment as an ongoing collaborative practice, pointing toward governance models where humans maintain critical capacity to redirect AI systems and where AI development remains answerable to diverse stakeholder values throughout deployment.
